title: Mapping Workflow
text: This page documents the unified mapping workflow (STAR, bwa, bowtie2) driven via ./scripts/bcfngs.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Overview

One workflow file: workflow/mapping.smk
One CLI entrypoint:

./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run

Default mapper: star
Default read mode: paired-end
Default bigWig conversion: bamCoverage --normalizeUsing CPM --binSize 10

Output layout:
<outdir>/<project>/<organism>/<mapper>/<sample>.bam
<outdir>/<project>/<organism>/<mapper>/<sample>.bam.bai
<outdir>/<project>/<organism>/<mapper>/bigwig/<sample>.bw
For STAR, the workflow also keeps:

<sample>.counts.tab
<sample>.SJ.out.tab

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Required configuration
Update configs/run.yaml:
project: "P000"
path: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/"
outdir: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/mapping"
organism: "Caenorhabditis_elegans"
threads: 16
ram_bytes: 168632718037
organism must be a single organism key from configs/genomes.yaml.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
FASTQ pattern auto-detection
The workflow detects input naming in this fixed order:

*_R1.fastq.gz
*_R1_001.fastq.gz
*_L1_R1.fastq.gz
*.conc.R1.fastq.gz

In paired-end mode, the corresponding R2 file must exist.

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
Basic usage
Dry-run first:
./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan
Run:
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run
Select mapper(s):
./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan --mapper star,bwa,bowtie2
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run  --mapper bwa
Read mode:
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run --single-end
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run --paired-end
Override config values for a single run:
./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan \
  --project P152 \
  --organism Drosophila_melanogaster \
  --fastq-dir /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P152/conc.fastq \
  --outdir /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/mapping
Keep intermediary files (default is cleanup):
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run --keep-intermediary-files
Tune bigWig options:
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run --bw-normalization CPM --bw-bin-size 10

title: Mapping Workflow
text:
SLURM execution
Run mapping on SLURM:
./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan --executor slurm --jobs 20
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run  --executor slurm --jobs 20
Defaults:

Partition: p.hpcl8
Mail user: yeroslaviz@biochem.mpg.de
Mail type: ALL
Time: 04:00:00

Key SLURM overrides:
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run \
  --executor slurm \
  --slurm-partition p.hpcl8 \
  --slurm-time 04:00:00 \
  --slurm-cpus-per-task 20 \
  --slurm-star-mem-mb 64000 \
  --slurm-aligner-mem-mb 32000
The wrapper sets per-rule resources so lightweight rules (indexing and bamCoverage) do not request mapper-level memory.

title: HOWTO
text: This is a short internal HOW-TO for the bcfngs mapping automation.

title: HOWTO
text:
1) Prepare environment
./scripts/bcfngs env create
./scripts/bcfngs env check
<<<<<<< Updated upstream 4. run the command snakemake -nps archive/legacy/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile to check that the new genome is added to the workflow (the -n is a dry-run which just output the command without performing the tasks.) 5. run the same command without the -n - snakemake -ps archive/legacy/getGenome_IndexGenome.Snakefile -j 12. This will download the fastA and gtf files from the given links and create the index files for them as well. The parameter -j sets the number pf cores used in the snakemake run. If the number of cores is smaller then the one sets for e.g. STAR, only this numner of cores will be used.

title: snakemake
text:
Configuration files

Genome configuration: configs/genomes.yaml
Per-run mapping configuration: configs/run.yaml

title: snakemake
text:
Genome workflow
Workflow file: workflow/genomes.smk
Examples:
<<<<<<< Updated upstream
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20

# optional explicit preflight (or use --no-skip-plan on build)
./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan
======= # all organisms ./scripts/bcfngs genomes build –cores 20 >>>>>>> Stashed changes

title: snakemake
text:
Genome download + indexing
Workflow file: workflow/genomes.smk

Mapping the samples with the STAR
mapping the samples in a paired-end format
This script contains three (four) steps. in the first step the fastq samples are mapped to the indexed genome. Then, they are being indexed. Last, after creating a chromosome-size file, the bam files are converted into bigwig files for better visualization using the UCSC browser.
We have two separate scripts for single-end and paired-end runs. The PE run looks for both R1 and R2 fastq files, while the SE script will ignore all R2 samples.
After the environment was created and activated, one must change to the directory of the concatenated fast files. E.g.
cd /fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P000/P000_Testrun/conc.fastq/
To run mapping, update configs/run.yaml in the repo, then run one of these Snakefiles.
# for single-end samples
snakemake -nps /path/to/repo/Star.MappingQuant.SingleEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50
# for paired-end samples
snakemake -nps /path/to/repo/Star.MappingQuant.PairedEndFastq.Snakefile -j 50

-n - dry-run. This parameter will only output the command, but execute them. This should be used to test the parameters, but must be removed for the analysis to be done.
-p - Print the commands to be execute.
-s - the name of the snakeake file to be executed.
-j - #cores. How many cores should be used in the run.
In paired-end mode, matching R2 files are required. >>>>>>> Stashed changes

title: index
text:
The repository contains a conda-snakemake workflow for the mapping of the concatenated fastq file created at the BCF of the MPIB.
This README file for the sequencing center to help them run a preliminary analysis on the sequenced samples
The file will contains instruction on how to create a conda environment with the needed tools for the mapping and will also include snakemake for the automation of the scripts.

title: index
text:
steps to do
Preparing the genomes for mapping

Download the genomes and the annotations from the FTP server (Ensembl genome builds are used here.)
For each genome, an index for the star aligner is created.

This two steps should be done only once, when the workflow was set up. It only needs to be re-run, when a new organism is being added, or when newer genome build of exiting organisms or a newer version of the STAR mapping tool are needed.
Mapping step

Map the samples with STAR, bwa and/or bowtie2 (parameter-controlled).
Index the BAM files (samtools index).
Convert BAM files directly to bigWig using deeptools bamCoverage.

points to consider: If this is only a preliminary analysis -

Would it make more sense to take only 50% of the fastq file for a faster analysis / visualization
Do they need the bam files also - deleting them would save a lot of storage space

The examples in this document are based on fictive data as well as a fictional project named Project0815. When a new project is done, this term must be changed with the proper name of the project, wherever it is mentioned.

title: index
text:
folder structure
The workflow is controlled via ./scripts/bcfngs and does not require changing directories for mapping.

Mapping and run settings are saved in configs/run.yaml.
Genome/index settings are saved in configs/genomes.yaml.
The mapping step auto-detects FASTQ naming patterns (*_R1.fastq.gz, *_R1_001.fastq.gz, *_L1_R1.fastq.gz, *.conc.R1.fastq.gz).
A unified mapping workflow handles both single-end and paired-end modes via command line flags.

title: index
text:
Workflow
The workflow uses a combination of a conda environment with the snakemake tool (based on python). The advantage of conda is that it creates a container, separated from the rest of the tools installed on the system. This gives a better management and control over the tools used in the workflow, as well as less conflict potentials with other/older version of tools with non-compatible parameters. To learn more about the conda environment and its advantages, take a look at the conda web site.
snakemake offers a lot of advantages as well. It allows better controls over different steps of the analysis, easier automation of the workflow and reduce running time through parallelization of the steps, just to mention some of them. to learn more about snakemake take a look at the snakemake doc site.

environment
snakemake


bigWig conversion
<<<<<<< Updated upstream bigWig generation is performed with deeptools bamCoverage directly from BAM input. ======= First the bam files are converted into wig files. This is done using the bam2wig.py script from the rseqc-software package.
In order for the automatic conversion of wig to bigwig files to work, one must have the tool wigToBigwig from the UCSC repository installed and in the path. As we are working with conda, the easiest place to put it is the path of the environment. In this project, the default environment path is /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes, so binaries are expected under /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes/bin/. So far I have copied the file manually to the folder, but I have now added it to the environment.yaml file, which is ran during the environment creation step. This was not tested yet!. >>>>>>> Stashed changes
TO DO
In this case it would probably be better if the indexed genomes would already be saved on the server for various organisms, as it doesn’t make sense to download one each time. We sort of know which organisms are needed in most cases.

human
mouse
zebra fish
fruit fly
yeast
worm (C. elegans)

as well as which mappers we need

star
bwa
bowtie2

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
codex/hpc-test: code and config only.
gh-pages: docs source + rendered site output only.

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
codex/hpc-test: code and config only.
gh-pages: docs source + rendered site output only.

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Allowed files by branch

codex/hpc-test examples:
scripts/
workflow/
configs/
env/
Snakefiles and helper code
gh-pages examples:
*.qmd, *.Rmd, _quarto.yml
docs/ rendered output

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Daily code workflow (strict split)
git switch codex/hpc-test
# edit code/config only
git add <code_or_config_files>
git commit -m "code: ..."
git push origin codex/hpc-test

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Daily docs publish workflow (strict split)
git switch gh-pages
git pull origin gh-pages
# edit docs source only
quarto render
git add *.qmd *.Rmd _quarto.yml docs/
git commit -m "docs: publish site update"
git push origin gh-pages

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
section: If docs were edited by mistake on codex/hpc-test
text:
If docs were edited by mistake on codex/hpc-test
Move docs work to gh-pages without committing docs on codex/hpc-test.
# 1) While on codex/hpc-test, stash docs-related files only
git stash push -u -m "move-docs-to-gh-pages" -- \
  index.Rmd conda.Rmd HOWTO.Rmd environment.qmd snakemake.Rmd updating_genomes.qmd \
  _quarto.yml docs/

# 2) Switch to docs branch and apply
git switch gh-pages
git pull origin gh-pages
git stash pop

# 3) Publish
quarto render
git add *.qmd *.Rmd _quarto.yml docs/
git commit -m "docs: publish site update"
git push origin gh-pages

# 4) Return to code branch
git switch codex/hpc-test

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Conflict note (docs/search.json)
If stash apply/pop conflicts on docs/search.json while on gh-pages:
git checkout --theirs docs/search.json
git add docs/search.json
Then continue staging/commit normally.

title: Branch and Publish Workflow (Local Notes)
text:
Non-negotiable rule

Never commit docs/ output to codex/hpc-test.
Never commit code/config pipeline changes to gh-pages.

title: Changelog
text:
Added unified mapping workflow: workflow/mapping.smk.
Added ./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan and ./scripts/bcfngs mapping run.
Added mapper selection via --mapper star,bwa,bowtie2 (default star).
Added unified read-mode selection via --paired-end (default) / --single-end.
Added FASTQ auto-detection with ordered pattern matching:

*_R1.fastq.gz
*_R1_001.fastq.gz
*_L1_R1.fastq.gz
*.conc.R1.fastq.gz

Added single-organism enforcement for mapping runs.
Replaced bam2wig.py conversion with deeptools bamCoverage.
Added --keep-intermediary-files (default behavior removes intermediaries).
Added mapping output layout:

<outdir>/<project>/<organism>/<mapper>/<sample>.bam
<outdir>/<project>/<organism>/<mapper>/<sample>.bam.bai
<outdir>/<project>/<organism>/<mapper>/bigwig/<sample>.bw

Added optional SLURM execution for mapping (--executor slurm) with per-rule resource defaults.
Added mapping documentation page: mapping.qmd.
Updated navigation and examples to reference mapping wrapper commands.
Archived legacy STAR-only Snakefiles to archive/legacy/mapping/.
Added outdir to configs/run.yaml.
Added deeptools dependency in env/environment.yml.

title: Updating genomes and indices
text: This page collects the internal procedures for:

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
When to update genomes / indices
Once every few years the genomic sequences must be updated. Also the STAR version for mapping progresses and should be updated when needed.

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
Prerequisites / where to run
You can use any server as long as you can run snakemake and the required tools. The recommended method is micromamba (see environment). Default environment path used on the cluster: /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
How-to (by scenario)

First time setup (recommended)
./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes env create
./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes env check


Routine: re-run indexing (no config changes)
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20

# optional explicit preflight (or use --no-skip-plan on build)
./scripts/bcfngs genomes plan


Only rebuild one or two organisms
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --organism Homo_sapiens,Mus_musculus


Ensembl published a new release

Check:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes check-updates

If an update is available, bump releases.ensembl in configs/genomes.yaml.
Then:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --no-skip-plan


You updated STAR/bwa/bowtie2 (rebuild indices into new versioned folders)

Update tools:

./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes env update
./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes env check

Update index_versions in configs/genomes.yaml (these strings are used in output folder names).
Rebuild:

./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20


HPC modules fallback (aligners from modules)
module load bclconvert/4.3.13 star bowtie2 bwa
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules env check
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes build --cores 20 --no-skip-plan


Recommended (micromamba)
./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes env create
./scripts/bcfngs --env-prefix /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes env check


HPC modules fallback (optional)
You can use modules for vendor tools:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13 star bowtie2 bwa

title: Updating genomes and indices
section: 1) Check for a new Ensembl release (report only)
text:
1) Check for a new Ensembl release (report only)
./scripts/bcfngs genomes check-updates

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
2) Update genome sources (if needed)
Genome sources are configured in configs/genomes.yaml. Organism identifiers use scientific names (e.g. Homo_sapiens, Mus_musculus).
To add or update a genome:

Add/update the organism entry under organisms:.
For Ensembl sources, keep releases.ensembl pinned (default: 116). Bumping the release is a controlled change.
Set sa_index appropriately for STAR indexing.

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
3) Build genomes + indices (safe workflow)
Build directly (default behavior skips preflight plan checks):
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20
Force preflight checks + lint + dry-run before build:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --no-skip-plan
Run through SLURM instead of local execution:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --executor slurm --jobs 20

# if SLURM executor is unavailable, refresh the environment first
./scripts/bcfngs env update
To build only a subset:
./scripts/bcfngs genomes build --cores 20 --organism Homo_sapiens,Mus_musculus

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
4) Versioned indices + symlinks
Indices are built into versioned directories (e.g. starIndex-<version>) and then symlinked so mapping can always use stable paths:

/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/starIndex
/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/bwaIndex
/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/genomes/<Organism>/bowtie2Index

Symlinks are updated automatically by the Snakemake workflow after successful index creation.

title: Updating genomes and indices
text:
5) Mapping (per project)
Mapping is now handled by a unified workflow (workflow/mapping.smk) and wrapper commands:

./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run

Supported mappers are STAR, bwa, and bowtie2 (selectable by --mapper).

Change parameters before mapping
Adjust configs/run.yaml:
project: "P152"
path: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/fastq_files/P152/conc.fastq/"
outdir: "/fs/pool/pool-bcfngs/mapping"
organism: "Drosophila_melanogaster"
threads: 16


Run mapping (dry-run first)
# default mapper and mode (STAR, paired-end)
./scripts/bcfngs mapping plan
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run

# choose mapper(s)
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run --mapper star,bwa,bowtie2

# single-end mode
./scripts/bcfngs mapping run --single-end
For more usage details, see mapping.qmd.

title: Environment (micromamba)
text: This workflow uses micromamba to create a reproducible environment with all required tools (Snakemake + aligners + utilities). Default environment path used for this project: /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Installing micromamba
micromamba can be installed from the github repository.
"${SHELL}" <(curl -L https://micro.mamba.pm/install.sh)

title: Environment (micromamba)
section: Create the workflow environment (default cluster path)
text:
Create the workflow environment (default cluster path)
From the repo root:
micromamba create -y -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes -f env/environment.yml
To run commands inside the environment without activating it:
micromamba run -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes snakemake --version

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Update the environment
If env/environment.yml changes (new tools, version bumps):
micromamba update -y -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes -f env/environment.yml

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
Health checks
./scripts/bcfngs env check
This step goes through the needed tools for the workflow and output if the tool versions. you should see something like that:
{
  "snakemake": "8.30.0",
  "STAR": "2.7.11b",
  "bwa": "",
  "bowtie2": "/fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes/bin/bowtie2-build-s version 2.5.5",
  "samtools": "samtools 1.23"
}

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
How-to (common scenarios)

I’m new / first time setup
micromamba create -y -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes -f env/environment.yml
./scripts/bcfngs env check


Run a one-off command without activating
micromamba run -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes snakemake --help
micromamba run -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes STAR --version


Keep tools up-to-date (routine)

Update the environment:

micromamba update -y -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes -f env/environment.yml
./scripts/bcfngs env check

(Optional) record an explicit lockfile for reproducibility:

mkdir -p env/locks
micromamba list -p /fs/gpfs41/lv07/fileset07/home/b_cox/yeroslaviz/miniconda3/envs/update_genomes --explicit > env/locks/explicit-YYYY-MM-DD.txt


I want to use HPC modules for vendor tools
You still need the micromamba env for python + snakemake, but aligners can come from modules:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13 star bowtie2 bwa
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules env check
./scripts/bcfngs --profile modules genomes build --cores 20 --no-skip-plan


Demultiplexing with bcl-convert (modules)
This repo doesn’t automate demultiplexing yet, but the typical pattern is:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13
bcl-convert --help

# Example placeholders (adjust to your run folder / SampleSheet):
# bcl-convert --bcl-input-directory /path/to/runfolder \
#            --output-directory /path/to/output \
#            --sample-sheet /path/to/SampleSheet.csv

title: Environment (micromamba)
text:
HPC modules fallback (optional)
If you prefer using HPC modules for vendor tools, you can load:
module load bclconvert/4.3.13 star bowtie2 bwa
You can still run Snakemake/python from micromamba (recommended).

title: conda
text:
The first step is to create a conda environment, containing the necessary tools for the required analysis. In this workflow, we use the snakemake package to help running the workflow, as well as some mappers. To learn more about the conda environment and its advantages, take a look at the conda web site.
Environments can be created with the help of a file, e.g. environment.yaml using the following command

conda env create --name Project0815 --file environment.yaml

This file would look like that:
name: Project0815
channels:
   - bioconda
   - conda-forge
   - r
   - bioinfo
dependencies:
   - snakemake-minimal =5.2.4
   - python =3.6
   - star =2.7.2c
   - samtools =1.9
   - bowtie2 =2.3.5
   - bwa =0.7.17
The first row of the file contains the name of the project, i.e. the name of the conda environment created. Next are the channels listed, from which the conda environment should look for the tools needed for the analysis.
the last part of the environment file is a list of the needed dependencies. Each one of them has its version attached to it. This can be very helpful, when a data set needs to be re-analyzed to check for problems or conflicts with older/newer results or for debugging purposes. In this workflow we will use the snakemake package as well as python version 3.6. we also install here the mappers star, bwa and bowtie2. WE also need the samtool package for the manipulation of the bam files (sorting, filtering, etc.).
After this was done one must activate the environment using the command

source activate Project0815

If one look at the command line one can now see this
(Project0815) USER@SERVERNAME:Project0815$ ..
The brackets on the left side must contains the name of the environment.
If, after the environment was created, new tools must be added, they can just be added to the list of tools in the environment.yaml file and than the conda environment must be updated, using the command

conda env update -f environment.yaml 

The conda environment should be created only once, but with each login one must activate it, so that the snakemake script can be run from the correct paths.
